posts.caption: Warning to anyone wanting to buy with Vestiaire…. Don’t do it. Bought a LV purse but instantly questioned authenticity (literally as soon as I purchased it) went straight on chat about this. They kindly offered to verify it. The purse came to me and it definitely appeared fake to me…. No heat stamp inside, no serial number, very thin cheap dust bag. Hardware not typical of LV. Sent it off for verification. Waited a few days and today I get a notification to say it’s been verified and is authentic…. 🫢 I do not believe it for a second. Go on to chat to discuss, they say it’s got a serial number (seller also told me no serial number as well as when I looked myself) so I asked where the serial number is and could I have a picture… bearing in mind now they have it they are refusing to refund me and they are listing it to be resold. They’ve said they can’t send me a picture and can’t provide me where it is. Which all makes no sense to me whatsoever. I’ve reminded them that me asking for that serial number is me asking for that of my property. I don’t believe it’s real and therefore don’t wish to keep it but it is mine. Asked to speak to a manager and denied. I mean I may get a refund if it sells in 2yrs? What if it doesn’t sell at all? I imagine that I won’t see that listing and really they will do anything with it because they have it in their possession even though it’s mine?? Absolutely vile company. I am a small buyer I’ve not spent thousands. I know me not purchasing will make no difference really but I did want to warn others. I will never use them again, I’m going to make a claim on PayPal and leave a trust pilot review…. It’s a good job I guess that it wasn’t something worth hundreds or thousands. Very very bad company.

posts.caption:
There was a point when female bodies were widely censored in media and online spaces, and platforms like Instagram regularly policed women’s nipples (but not men’s, of course). The fight against their censorship quickly became a larger conversation about bodily autonomy, sexuality, and politics, and that same rhetoric has played out in high fashion as well, where the nipple is both a political statement and design motif.
This year’s Met Gala theme, ‘Costume Art’, offered plenty of room to highlight the relationship between clothing and the body—what we cover and what we expose, what we transform versus what we celebrate. All eyes were on the red carpet, where the three Kardashian sisters seemed to have received the same memo: bare your boobies. Add to the mix Alex Consani (@alexconsani) in a sheer Gucci (@gucci) corset and Cardi B’s (@iamcardib) comical padded boob clusters and the secondary theme to the gala was quite palpable.
At the link in bio, @tanya.91 on how the 2026 Met Gala proved that breasts have moved from taboo to couture.

There were gowns, there were capes, there were (quite literally) extra limbs on the carpet, but the real scene-stealer at this year’s Met Gala wasn’t stitched, draped, or even bedazzled. It was…a mango. Yes, that mango. The one you kept zooming into like a true detective. The one clutched inside businesswoman and philanthropist Isha Ambani’s bag. And no, unlike what most of us thought, it wasn’t a cheeky snack between photo ops—it was a sculpture by New Delhi-based artist Subodh Gupta(@subodhguptastudio) and arguably the most deliciously ironic accessory on a carpet where the dress code for the night was ‘Fashion is Art’.

posts.caption:
There’s no official prize for best dressed at the Met Gala, but if there were, Robert Wun(@robertwun) would’ve swept it. The London College of Fashion graduate founded his namesake label in 2014, later pivoting exclusively to couture. Today, he is known for combining razor-sharp tailoring with imaginative silhouettes and a cinematic, otherworldly approach to dressmaking. In January 2023, Wun made history as the first designer from Hong Kong to join the official Paris Haute Couture calendar as a guest member. He has since created custom couture looks for everyone from Adele and Ariana Grande to Lady Gaga and Priyanka Chopra.
It’s no surprise, then, that his fantastical creations ended up on some of the best-dressed guests at this year’s Met Gala. At the link in bio, we revisit each of his looks from the night. Spoiler alert: not a single miss.

posts.caption:
Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.

posts.caption: To give more context for those who may not know, the MET gala is a fundraiser for the costume department of the Metropolitan Museum of art in New York, "The MET." The costume department of the MET does not receive any taxpayer dollars, it is completely funded by private donation. As this is a charity event, they need to have attendees who can donate or raise big funds. By getting celebrities to come, they get a lot of media attention. It is an excellent formula for funding this branch of the museum. The Museums in the USA are funded by American citizens, financiers, captains of industry, and artists coming together as is the case with the MET. Everyone who lives in New York City can visit the Metropolitan museum of art for free. It is a fantastic museum, the building is is 2.2 million square feet and yet can only show a portion of it's inventory at one time. The museum has way more than paintings and dresses. It has sculptures, Persian rugs, various pieces of historical and religious art, and a glorious collection of furniture. Although America has many museums, most of them pale in comparison to what you see in Europe. But the MET is one of our crown jewels. It is very expensive to operate and preserve. This type of fundraiser helps to prevent tax burden on the citizens of New York. And yes, I love the MET.

posts.caption:
A few days ago I posted about Mariano Fortuny and promised a full post on Henriette, because she deserved the spotlight rather than the margin.
This is that post.
Everything here traces to a single document…a patent filed in Paris on 10 June 1909, held at the Marciana Library in Venice. Mariano filed it under his name because he was present and the deadline required it. Then he wrote a note on the same document, in his own handwriting, naming Henriette Negrin as the inventor.
That note sat in the archive for 112 years but the V&A only updated their catalogue labels in 2021.
Mariano’s acknowledgement was there from the beginning…it just took the institution a century to follow it.

posts.caption:
Yesterday I posted about the Indus Valley, where cotton was first threaded through copper beads in 6000 BCE, where the draped rectangle became the primary garment of one of the largest civilisations in the ancient world.
That logic travelled west through Mesopotamia, Egypt and Greece. In 1909 it arrived in a palazzo on the Grand Canal in the hands of a man who rowed himself across the Venetian lagoon every morning to make gowns that could not be dated.
Mariano Fortuny called himself a painter but else called him the Magician of Venice. He was severely allergic to horses, obsessed with electricity and spent his life reaching for a father he lost at three years old.
Peggy Guggenheim wore the same Delphos for decades. Isadora Duncan wore it to revive Classical Greek dance. Marcel Proust used it as a symbol of desire, memory, and lost time throughout one of the greatest novels ever written.
The Met @metmuseum chose it to announce this year’s theme. Fashion is Art and Fortuny already knew.
He was reaching for ancient Greece but the tradition he was reviving is older than Greece. It goes back to the Indus Valley and to the origin of cloth.
Part of an ongoing series on fashion, history, and whose stories get told.

posts.caption:
Last week I talked about Mesopotamia where cloth was the economy, where 6,000 women worked the textile workshops of Girsu and where cuneiform may have developed in part to track the production of thread.
That story travelled east.
The Indus Valley Civilisation. Modern-day Pakistan, northwest India, northeast Afghanistan. Larger than ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia combined at its peak. And the oldest confirmed source of cotton in the Old World, evidence going back to 6000 BCE, a full millennium before the Harappan cities even existed.
Fashion history mostly starts in Egypt. Sometimes Mesopotamia. The Indus Valley predates both for cotton, was larger than both at its peak, and produced the draped garment that over a billion people still wear today.
Three reasons it dropped out of the story: the script has never been deciphered. The major excavations were conducted by British colonial administrators in the 1920s. After partition in 1947, the two largest sites ended up in Pakistan while most of the scholarly infrastructure remained in India.
The one I keep finding when I look east instead of west. This is part of an ongoing series on fashion, history, and whose stories get told.

posts.caption:
😍 Gigi Hadid at the Met Gala: A Deep Dive
​This time, Gigi appeared in a yellow floral gown by Thom Browne, which was hand-stitched for nearly 13,500 hours. You aren't the only one who found Gigi Hadid’s appearance this year a bit "boring," but fashion critics and major magazines (such as Vogue and Harper's Bazaar) still included her on their "Best Dressed" lists regardless.
​Gigi Hadid currently sits around the middle of the Top 10 rankings. Here is a more detailed breakdown of her standing:
​Technically — "Top-Tier"
​The Thom Browne gown Gigi wore is considered a true work of art. More than 70 people worked on it for over 13,500 hours. The dress weighed approximately 18 kg (40 lbs), and Gigi required five assistants just to help her move. Experts highly praised it as a pinnacle of Haute Couture.
​On Theme — "Perfect, but Predictable"
​She followed the "The Garden of Time" theme 100%. Her dress was meant to evoke garden statues and overgrown roses. However, because she didn't deliver the "drama" of Zendaya or the "innovation" of Tyla (with her sand dress), she slipped to third or fourth place in public polls.
​"Old Hollywood" Style — "The Safe Choice"
​Gigi took a very classic route this time to maintain her "Supermodel" status. While critics called her the "epitome of elegance," for fans on social media, she was somewhat overshadowed by Zendaya’s two distinct looks and Janelle Monáe’s futuristic ensemble 😍

It’s a new era for @APC_paris. The French brand has appointed @LudivinePoiblanc as artistic director. Poiblanc is a stylist and creative director best known for her contributions to major publications like @WSJMag and advising fashion houses on product and visual identity. In the new role, she will shape the brand’s “image and visual universe”, according to a statement. The creative shift comes three years after a change in ownership.
“Not only has Ludivine shown her genuine talent as a stylist through her impressive career, but her personality, personal style, and wit make me confident that she will embody the brand I founded in 1987, and write a new chapter to A.P.C.’s adventure, respectful of the past and imaginative for the future,” Jean Touitou said, who has been leading the brand with his wife Judith Touitou, A.P.C.’s artistic director for nearly 30 years. @LaureGuilbault has the details at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
What happens when you put Meryl Streep and Anna Wintour—the two Mirandas, as it were—in a room together? A wide-ranging conversation about fashion, family, friendship, and yes, the forthcoming sequel to “The Devil Wears Prada.”
“In terms of Miranda, and coming back to that character 20 years later, I did think honestly about Anna, and tried to imagine what it was like to carry her responsibility and to be as interested in the world and curious as she must have to be,” Streep tells Greta Gerwig in Vogue’s May 2026 cover story. And what did Wintour do when she heard the rumours that “The Devil Wears Prada 2” was happening? She called Streep, of course. “I knew she would tell me if it was going to be all right,” Wintour says. “I trusted her implicitly.”

Never one to make a timid entrance, @Madonna made a big statement on the 2026 #MetGala red carpet. There was the witchy dress by @ysl, the tumble of raven hair, and the seven ladies-in-waiting carrying her diaphonous lavender cape, a reinterpretation of the 1945 oil painting “The Temptation of St. Anthony,” by one of the singer’s favorite artists, the British-Mexican Surrealist Leonora Carrington. The hat once belonged to the infamous fashion editor Isabella Blow, and was commissioned by her from fashion’s go-to milliner, Philip Treacy, who says it’s among his favorite creations.

posts.caption: If the Met’s new exhibition, “Costume Art”  is about ideas, the fashion at the 2026 #MetGala was about references—big, bold, occasionally delightfully on-the-nose ones. While @GracieAbrams shimmered straight out of Klimt’s Portrait of Adele Bloch-Bauer I, @RachelZegler delivered full historical drama by way of The Execution of Lady Jane Grey. @Madonna didn’t just reference Leonora Carrington, she channeled her—entourage and all. posts.caption:
It’s a new era for @APC_paris. The French brand has appointed @LudivinePoiblanc as artistic director. Poiblanc is a stylist and creative director best known for her contributions to major publications like @WSJMag and advising fashion houses on product and visual identity. In the new role, she will shape the brand’s “image and visual universe”, according to a statement. The creative shift comes three years after a change in ownership.
“Not only has Ludivine shown her genuine talent as a stylist through her impressive career, but her personality, personal style, and wit make me confident that she will embody the brand I founded in 1987, and write a new chapter to A.P.C.’s adventure, respectful of the past and imaginative for the future,” Jean Touitou said, who has been leading the brand with his wife Judith Touitou, A.P.C.’s artistic director for nearly 30 years. @LaureGuilbault has the details at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
It's a big year for @maudeapatow. Aside from returning to #Euphoria, the actress is stepping into her feature directorial debut with Poetic License. "I'm glad I didn't do this earlier," she says of directing. "I don't think I would have been ready." The tone of the film is occasionally enigmatic, brought to life by the particular relationships within it, with a cast that includes Apatow's mother, Leslie Mann. "Working on the script with my mom in mind and rewriting it, I just wanted it to feel like what I knew but amplified and weirder," Apatow says of bringing a mother-daughter relationship to life on screen.
Read more on Apatow's directorial era in our May cover story at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
Watches and Wonders really lived up to the second half of its name this year.
The annual expo in Geneva, Switzerland, brings together the watch industry’s biggest players to unveil their most wondrous, whimsical, and world-class creations.
Last year, I attended the convention in person, where I got to try on all the new watches by Chopard, Cartier, TAG Heuer, Piaget, and more. Not many people can say they wore something before it graced the steps of the Met Gala, but that was exactly the case with a new Cartier Panthère design I tried on at Watches and Wonders in 2025 that debuted on Jon Batiste on the famed red carpet a month later.
#womenculture #womenwatch #luxurywatch

Inspired by armor, art and gothic Victorian silhouettes, the actress swapped tailored dressing for something more sculptural and ethereal for fashion’s biggest night.

posts.caption:
@tessamaethompson is entering her blue era — and doing it on the Met steps.
Inspired by Yves Klein’s signature hue, the actress worked with @maisonvalentino creative director Alessandro Michele on a custom gown that blurs the line between fashion and fine art.
“I’ve never really worn blue on a carpet,” she said while getting ready. “But it’s a color I’ve been really attracted to recently… I love this idea of the dress almost feeling like paint.”
After an eight-show Broadway week, Thompson spent her one day off preparing for fashion’s biggest night — the Met Gala.
Read more at WWD.com — #linkinbio

posts.caption:
A'ja Wilson's custom gilded atelier @prabalgurung gown for the 2026 Met Gala carpet emulated the WNBA superstar's strength and determination.
“This is my first Met; the second one I’ve been invited to, so I’m blessed for the opportunity. We had a pre-season game last night; just stopped by New York on the first Monday of May, and will be right back out there tomorrow because our season starts. We get our [championship] rings this weekend. It’s an exciting week,” Wilson told WWD while getting ready at The Plaza hotel.
See more inside her glam session at WWD.com — #linkinbio
